questions.q: The bisection method guarantees convergence to a root if the function is continuous on [a,b] and f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs. What is the convergence rate of bisection?
options: Quadratic (second-order)
Linear (first-order)
Superlinear
Cubic (third-order)
questions.explanation: Bisection has linear convergence with rate 0.5 per iteration; the error is halved each step. Although guaranteed to work, it converges slowly compared to Newton-Raphson which has quadratic convergence.

questions.q: Which condition is NOT required for Newton-Raphson method to converge quadratically near a simple root x*?
options: f''(x) exists and is bounded near x*
Initial guess x₀ must be sufficiently close to x*
f(x) must be defined everywhere on ℝ
questions.explanation: Newton-Raphson only needs f and f' to be defined in a neighborhood of the root, not on all of ℝ. The other conditions are essential: f'(x*) ≠ 0 ensures the root is simple, f'' bounds the curvature controlling convergence, and x₀ must be in the basin of attraction.

questions.q: In Newton-Raphson iteration x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n), what happens geometrically?
options: The tangent line to f at x_n is used; x_{n+1} is where it crosses the x-axis
A secant line connects f(x_n) and f(x_{n-1})
The next iterate is the midpoint between x_n and f(x_n)
A parabola is fitted through three recent iterates
questions.explanation: Newton-Raphson draws the tangent line at (x_n, f(x_n)) and finds its x-intercept. This geometric interpretation shows why it converges so fast for well-behaved functions. The secant method uses two prior points instead.

questions.q: The secant method differs from Newton-Raphson in that it:
options: Avoids computing derivatives by approximating f'(x_n) ≈ [f(x_n) - f(x_{n-1})] / (x_n - x_{n-1})
Requires analytical derivatives but converges faster
Uses bisection to narrow the interval
Always requires an even number of iterations
questions.explanation: The secant method uses a finite difference approximation of the derivative, making it useful when f'(x) is hard to compute. Its convergence rate is superlinear (≈1.618) but slower than Newton's quadratic rate.

questions.q: Which statement about convergence rates is correct?
options: Linear convergence means |e_{n+1}| ≤ C|e_n| for some constant C < 1
Quadratic convergence requires fewer iterations than linear convergence
Both linear and quadratic convergence achieve the same final accuracy
The order of convergence is independent of the method's design
questions.explanation: Quadratic convergence (|e_{n+1}| ≤ C|e_n|²) dramatically reduces iterations near the solution. Linear convergence (|e_{n+1}| ≤ C|e_n|) is much slower. For example, quadratic convergence can double correct digits per iteration, while linear convergence adds roughly one digit per 3-4 iterations.

questions.q: Why does Newton-Raphson fail or converge slowly when f'(x*) = 0 at the root x*?
options: The root is multiple and the denominator becomes very large, causing near-singular behavior
The tangent line becomes horizontal so the x-intercept is undefined
The initial guess must be even closer to x*, losing quadratic convergence
Numerical rounding errors dominate the computation
questions.explanation: When f'(x*) = 0, the root is multiple (multiplicity > 1). Newton-Raphson reverts to linear convergence or worse. For a double root, modified Newton-Raphson with x_{n+1} = x_n - 2f(x_n)/f'(x_n) restores quadratic convergence.

questions.q: The fixed-point iteration x_{n+1} = g(x_n) converges if |g'(x*)| < 1 near the root x* = g(x*). What does |g'(x*)| ≈ 0 indicate?
options: Very fast (superlinear) convergence
The iteration is unstable and diverges
Exactly one iteration is sufficient
The method becomes a bisection step
questions.explanation: When |g'(x*)| is very small, convergence accelerates. For Newton-Raphson written as x_{n+1} = x_n - f(x_n)/f'(x_n), g'(x*) = 1 - f''(x*)/f'(x*) near simple roots, which can be very small, yielding quadratic convergence.

questions.q: Starting with the interval [0, 2], how many bisection iterations are needed to reduce the error below 10^{-4}?
options: Approximately 14 iterations
Approximately 7 iterations
Approximately 20 iterations
Approximately 4 iterations
questions.explanation: Bisection error halves each iteration: e_n = (b-a)/2^{n+1}. With interval width 2, we need 2/2^{n+1} < 10^{-4}, so 2^{n+1} > 20000. Taking log₂: n+1 > 14.3, thus n ≈ 14 iterations.

questions.q: Why is the secant method considered preferable to Newton-Raphson in some practical applications?
options: It avoids computing f'(x) which may be unavailable or expensive
It has higher convergence order than Newton-Raphson
It never requires an initial guess for x₀
It always stays within the original bracketing interval
questions.explanation: The main advantage is avoiding derivative computation. While secant has slower superlinear convergence (≈1.618) versus Newton's quadratic, it uses only two function evaluations per step versus one function and one derivative for Newton.

questions.q: A root-finding algorithm fails to converge starting from x₀ = 0.1 for f(x) = x² - 1 near x = 1. Which scenario is most likely?
options: The method was Newton-Raphson and f'(1) = 0
The method was bisection but no sign change was bracketed
The method was Newton-Raphson and initial guess was outside the basin of attraction
The function has no root near x = 1
questions.explanation: For f(x) = x² - 1, we have f'(1) = 2 ≠ 0, so (a) is false. Newton-Raphson from x₀ = 0.1 converges to x = 1. Bisection only works if f(a) and f(b) have opposite signs; if both endpoints have the same sign, bisection fails immediately. For this function, the function values are f(a) and f(b) are both negative or positive depending on the interval.
